editorialNotes.standard: As the vibrato within the Bee Gees’ vocal triumvirate, Robin Gibb helped set the balance between brother Barry’s falsetto and twin brother Maurice’s harmonizing. His lead on early tracks such “I Started a Joke,” “Massachusetts,” and “I’ve Gotta Get a Message to You” exemplified the band’s unique blend of British Invasion immediacy and soft-pop perfection in their first incarnation. As the band grew into a disco powerhouse in the ’70s, Robin was an essential part of the harmonies that spread across the dance floors of the world. The Bee Gees’ place in the pantheon of pop music’s greats is secure thanks in large part to Robin Gibb’s talents as a gifted songwriter and vocalist.
editorialNotes.short: Bee Gees’ Barry Gibb joins Zane to discuss the trio’s rise from pop balladeers to disco kings.
Catch a glimpse of the Apple Original Film, an intimate look at the making of Letter To You.

editorialNotes.standard: If rock 'n' roll truly does have a king, his name is Chuck Berry. This man defined the strut, the finger-wagging talk, the riff, and the dirty, low-down funk of early rock 'n' roll. He was a duck-walking maniac on stage; his eyes crazed and his fingers letting out guitar leads that were mixing up rhythms nicked from jump blues and boogie-woogie. But he wasn't just a stylist, he was a songwriter - knocking out original tunes that were rampant with salacious imagery and quick-witted rhymes. His immediately recognizable skeletal R&B-tinged rave-ups and gritty, greasy guitar work is still the template for rock in its purest form. All hail Chuck Berry.

attributes.name: desert sand feels warm at night
desert sand feels warm at night
Digital Farm Animals, Shaun Frank & Dragonette

editorialNotes.short: Foo Fighters look back on 25 years of hits and the stories behind them with Zane.

editorialNotes.name: Gorillaz
editorialNotes.standard: Laconic trip-hop beats skate beneath ex-Blur frontman Damon Albarn’s distinctive Brit vocals on the animated foursome’s debut album. Nods to hip-hop futurism on “New Genius (Brother)” and dub swagger on “Slow Country” are fused together by Kid Koala’s skittering scratching and Dan "The Automator" Nakamura’s sizzling electronics.
editorialNotes.short: Laconic trip-hop beats and sizzling electronics from Albarn and co.
Sept. 23 at 3:30 p.m. ET / 12:30 p.m. PT

editorialNotes.standard: Louis “Satchmo” Armstrong was not just an outstanding musician but also a humanitarian whose contributions to jazz and culture put him on the level of innovators like Shakespeare, Picasso, and Ellington. Louis Armstrong began playing cornet before switching to the trumpet, an instrument he would master and be inextricably linked with, blessing jazz with a legacy of timeless recordings such as “Hello Dolly,” “A Kiss to Build a Dream On,” “Heebie Jeebies,” “Mack the Knife,” and the internationally recognized ode to optimism, “What a Wonderful World.” Satchmo’s fingerprints are all over popular music; he introduced now-standard innovations such as soloing and scatting. Discover the many sides of Louis Armstrong presented here (as he closed every correspondence) “red beans and ricely yours.”
